chapter.title: Overview
chapter.simpara: QALIPSIS is an enterprise-grade load and performance-testing tool especially designed for distributed systems. It is a step above other load test applications that merely evaluate your system internally.

listitem.simpara: QALIPSIS is developer-centric with a low learning curve that makes it easy to use and extremely efficient.
QALIPSIS is developed in Kotlin to gain the benefits of the Java ecosystem and provide seamless system performance.
The basic out-of-the box QALIPSIS tool is completely free of charge.
Commercial-licensed-based plugins, the cloud-based application, and other premium extensions are not included in the basic out-of-the box QALIPSIS tool.
QALIPSIS is designed to test the performance of distributed and monolithic systems.
QALIPSIS collects data from operating systems, databases, and monitoring tools.
QALIPSIS cross-checks system metrics and performs data verification to ensure that the test systems is performing as expected.

section.title: User-friendly interface
section.simpara: The QALIPSIS Graphical User Interface (GUI) allows users, depending on their role, to configure and run load tests with capabilities including, but not limited to:

listitem.simpara: Start/Stop
Configure the Load Distribution
Increase/Decrease the Number of Simulated Users
View Reports
Schedule a Test

section.title: Automation friendly performance verification
section.simpara: Using the Command Line Interface (CLI) or the Gradle plugin, you can automatically and repeatedly verify the condition and performance of your system.

section.title: Developer friendly plug-ins
section.simpara: Downloading a ready-to-use project for your IDE is made easier on QALIPSIS, consequently integrating with popular DevOps tools to test your software and track the impact of recent changes.

section.title: Aggregated assertions
section.simpara: Aggregated assertions allow you to verify the overall results on a scenario. You can define thresholds to make a test succeed or fail and immediately get insights about the performance and consistency of your tested system.

listitem.simpara: QALIPSIS can test distributed systems.
QALIPSIS can perform as a distributed system.
QALIPSIS can simulate load from several geographic locations to run comparisons based on location.

section.title: Live results
section.simpara: QALIPSIS events and meters provide extended details of load-test operations, providing users with various options for accessing the results. The results visualizations and dashboards, with details of each test-case execution, provide valuable insight for identifying the bottlenecks in your applications and uncovering the components and conditions of failures.

section.title: Concurrent load simulations
section.simpara: QALIPSIS simulates the load from thousands to millions of users (e.g., website visitors, connected IoT devices, etc.) and cross-verifies successive values from different sources including requests, responses, infrastructure metrics, messaging platforms and databases.

section.title: Operating system independent
section.simpara: QALIPSIS load and performance testing can be performed from any operating system supporting Java with the same impressive results.

chapter.title: Benefits
chapter.simpara: QALIPSIS was designed with distributed systems and current developer practices in mind. It provides a testing framework to allow developers to work in a real-world environment, writing and running load tests without a steep learning curve.

section.title: For developers
section.simpara: QALIPSIS empowers developers to:

listitem.simpara: Choose their own testing tools and preferred programming language for writing load-test scenarios.

Cross-verify data from different sources: bring responses to requests, infrastructure metrics, middleware events and database records in the same port to compare them and verify that they match your expectations.
Unlock the full power of the Java ecosystem by making use of any library, protocol or system.
Reuse pieces of their own software to faster develop scenarios.
Simulate users, third-party software and IoT devices.
Simulate real-user and device behavior to make load tests more relevant.
Generate detailed and relevant results by using distributed tracing markers to follow the complete execution workflow from end to end.
Customize reports and dashboards.
Perform tests on development machines, containers and virtual machines with excellent results.
Integrate their standard DevOps workflow for performance testing before the deployment of health checks on production environments.
Create a scenario in just a few minutes by enabling utilization of any Java library of choice (such as JUnit, Hamcrest, AssertJ) or pieces of software or proprietary libraries.
Test non-http technologies using numerous plug-ins, such as databases and messaging platforms.

section.title: For IT operations teams
section.simpara: QALIPSIS provides IT Operations teams solutions to:

listitem.simpara: Monitor how deployments and other IT operations can affect the availability and overall performance of the system.
Expose bottlenecks and find correlations between load and resource consumption by unifying the verification of load-test and infrastructure metrics.
Identify issues before users and customers do by verifying the size and configuration of the system’s infrastructure.
Examine how the system performs during normal and peak loads by running load tests from different geographic locations at varying times.
Generate exceptional results on virtual engines, containers and conventional development machines.
Allow for implementation and integration of verifications in their standard DevOps workflow.

partintro.simpara: QALIPSIS 1.0 is the initial product release.
QALIPSIS 1.0 is designed with the scalability and simplicity required to perform load-tests and performance verification on both distributed and stand-alone systems

listitem.simpara: You can create scenarios with different branches and running with many minions.
You can use its many native steps and plugins to fire load on your system and feed QALIPSIS with data to verify load strength.

partintro.simpara: QALIPSIS 1.0 runs standalone and distributed. A history of events and metrics can be logged or saved in Elasticsearch, TimescaleDB and InfluxDB. Plugins for other tools will be available in future releases.
Future releases of QALIPSIS will include more plugins, more steps for load injection and verifications as well as other improvements.
Feedback on this release, including bugs and suggested enhancements, is welcome and may be sent to the QALIPSIS team at {link to the GitHub issues page}.

chapter.title: Overview
chapter.simpara: Once you have a minimal understanding of the core concepts, you will be ready to create a QALIPSIS scenario to verify that your software is performing as expected.
The aim of QALIPSIS is to execute actions that inject load into IT systems to monitor and validate their behaviors (latency, failure rates, availability, and data consistency).

The actions are performed by minions that inject load into a system and then run through a preconfigured scenario consisting of steps and assertions. If running QALIPSIS on a distributed system, you can create clusters with Head and Factory nodes to inject higher load.
Before attempting to configure QALIPSIS to run on your system, it is important to understand the core concepts of the tool:

listitem.simpara: Minions
Scenarios
Steps
Assertions
Head
Factory

chapter.title: Minions
chapter.simpara: Minions are simulations of website users, IoT devices, or other systems. They each perform a complete scenario.
Any number of minions can act on a scenario. The minion count for a scenario is set during scenario configuration (refer to ) or at runtime (refer to ).
Note that a minion count entered at runtime overrides the minion count set during scenario configuration.

chapter.title: Scenarios
chapter.simpara: A QALIPSIS scenario is the single largest component of a load test. It contains steps with a detailed description of all the operations to accomplish, their dependencies, as well as assertions to ensure everything performs as expected.
A simple scenario configuration includes the following:

listitem.simpara: Call to the scenario function.
Mandatory
QALIPSIS Signature: @Scenario
Assign the scenario a name.
Mandatory
Value: alphanumeric string
QALIPSIS Signature: val myScenario = scenario("scenario name")
Define the total # of minions to be executed.
Optional
Value: positive number
Default = 1; Minimum = 1
QALIPSIS Signature: minionsCount = xxxxx
Define the ramping strategy.
Mandatory
QALIPSIS Signature: rampUp {strategy( )}
Define the tree where the load will be injected.
Mandatory
QALIPSIS Signature: start()
Call the Gradle Plugin for QALIPSIS.
Mandatory
listitem.programlisting: plugins { application kotlin("jvm") kotlin("kapt") id("com.github.johnrengelman.shadow") version "6.0.0" id("com.palantir.docker") }

listitem.simpara: Add steps to your scenario.

chapter.title: Steps
chapter.simpara: Steps are the central components for working with QALIPSIS and can be developed in Kotlin using the fluent API.
Steps depend on data sources, results of other steps or assertions to execute or use their data.
A step specification is configured such that:

listitem.simpara: The step receives the configuration closure as a parameter.
.configure is called after the step parameters are set.

chapter.simpara: Refer to the section of this manual for details.
Each step has a unique configuration depending on the purpose of the step. The step configurations all follow the same logic:

listitem.simpara: Declared as a root or successor step.
Provided a name.
Provided with step context.
Delivers output data.

chapter.simpara: Steps are configured using core operators:

listitem.simpara: Assertions
Timing
Creating
Conditional
Converting
Error Handling
Filtering
Joining
Mathematical/Aggregate
Transforming
Flow
User-defined

chapter.simpara: Refer to the section of this manual for details.

chapter.title: Assertions
chapter.simpara: Assertions perform cross-checks within the scenario by:

listitem.simpara: Providing validation of data persistence, message exchanges, and requests to remote systems.
Setting and testing durations to verify the system’s overall responsiveness.

chapter.title: Nodes
chapter.simpara: Nodes are distributed instances within a QALIPSIS cluster. QALIPSIS nodes are designated as either a Head or a Factory.

section.title: Head
section.simpara: The head node of a QALIPSIS cluster is responsible for orchestration and reporting.

section.title: Factory
section.simpara: The factory node of a QALIPSIS cluster hosts the minions that will execute the load tests and assertions.

part.title: Quickstart guide: Testing an HTTP server with QALIPSIS

partintro.simpara: In this quickstart guide, you will learn how to test a very simple echo-like HTTP service using QALIPSIS.

chapter.title: Before you get started
chapter.simpara: Before starting, you need:

listitem.simpara: A general understanding of the .
Java 11 SDK or above installed on your system.
Docker installed on your system.

chapter.title: What this guide covers
chapter.simpara: This guide will take you through the following steps for testing a simple HTTP service:

listitem.simpara: Start an HTTP server and a docker environment for the project.
Generate a project archive.
Configure a scenario skeleton.
Add steps to the scenario.
An HTTP step to send requests to the server.
A verify step that asserts that the server’s response is "OK" and to verify the server’s responses.
An HTTP WITH step that reuses the HTTP connection.
A second assertion step to verify the server’s responses.
Execute the scenario using Gradle and view the report.
Create a Java archive of your project so that you can transport it as needed and run it anywhere.
to allow you to view errors in the report.
Create a file named docker-compose.yml in your working directory with the following content:
listitem.programlisting: version: '3.5' services: http-punching-ball: image: aerisconsulting/http-punching-ball command: - "--https=true" - "--ssl-key=http-server.key" - "--ssl-cert=http-server.crt" ports: - "18080:8080" - "18443:8443"

listitem.simpara: Start the created docker environment with the command docker compose up -d.

chapter.title: Create a project

listitem.simpara: In your browser, open the website Bootstrap QALIPSIS.
Verify that your system has the pre-requisites listed on the Bootstrap QALIPSIS page.
In the "Name" field, enter "Quickstart".
In the "plugins" menu, select netty.
In the assertions libraries menu, select Kotest.
Click Generate. An archive containing a skeleton of the project is proposed for download.
Download the proposed project archive.

chapter.simpara: [ insert graphic/screenshot of the Bootstrap QALIPSIS page ]

section.title: Edit the downloaded scenario skeleton

listitem.simpara: Open the downloaded project archive in your IDE.
Edit the file src/main/kotlin/Quickstart.kt.

section.simpara: The scenario skeleton should be displayed:

section.title: Configure the scenario basics
section.simpara: Configure the scenario to inject 10,000 minions at a rate of 100 minions every 500 ms.

section.title: Add steps to the scenario
section.simpara: Add and configure steps within the scenario that will send an HTTP request to the server and verify that the server’s response is as expected.

section.title: Send an HTTP request
section.simpara: Add an http step to the scenario that will send a POST request to the server.

callout.para: Specifies the starting point for load injection.
Gives the http step a name.
Configures the HTPP connection to the server.
Configures the HTTP request (HTTP method, path and body) that will be sent to the server.
Enables reporting of errors for the http step.

section.simpara: Execute the scenario using Gradle: ./gradlew run. This allows you to observe requests logged by the HTTP server. However, it does not verify that the server is returning what you expect.
[ insert screen shot here of the report]

section.title: Verify the server’s response
section.simpara: To verify that the server is returning what you expect, add a verify step to the scenario, just after the http step.

callout.para: Place holder for the http step (quickstart-http-post) configuration parameters.
Verifies that the HTTP status of the response is OK; the step uses io.netty.handler.codec.http.HttpResponseStatus for the verification.
Verifies that the body of the response contains the string ("Hello World!") sent in the request.

section.simpara: At this point, if you execute the updated scenario, a log file will display showing the status of each response and confirmation that that server is posting the "Hello World!" string.

insert.title: Reuse the HTTP connection
insert.simpara: You can strengthen the relevance of your test by:

listitem.simpara: Adding an httpWith step to the scenario that reuses the original HTTP connection (quickstart-http-post).
The same minions that passed through the first http step will pass through the httpWith step.
Adding a second verify step to assert that the response from the server is as expected.

callout.para: Calls the httpWith operator and specifies the previous http connection to reuse ("quickstart-http-post").
callout.simpara: Note that there is no need to specify parameters for the`httpWith` connection since it reuses the parameters from the previous http connection.

callout.para: Verifies that the HTTP status of the response is OK; the step uses io.netty.handler.codec.http.HttpResponseStatus for the verification.
Verifies that the body of the response contains the string ("Hello World!") sent in the request.
Not sure I understand what this line of code does…​

section.title: Execute the scenario
section.simpara: Execute your updated scenario as a Gradle project.
A couple of seconds after execution, a report (similar to the one below) will display on your console.

listitem.simpara: Note that the information for the whole campaign as well as for each scenario is displayed.
In the example above, there are errors, which explains why the execution failed.
To understand the errors, refer to Enable Event Logging .

section.title: Create an archive of your scenario
section.simpara: Execute the statement ./gradlew assemble.

A JAR archive ending with -qalipsis.jar is created in the folder build/libs. This archive contains all dependencies and is self-sufficient to execute your scenario on any machine with a Java Runtime Environment installed.

To execute your scenario: . Open a terminal and navigate to the folder where the JAR archive is stored. . Move the JAR archive out of the build folder; this will avoid accidental deletion of the archive. . Execute the command java -jar quickstart-<your-version>-qalipsis.jar.

section.title: Enable event logging
section.simpara: To know the details of the failures In current directory, create a folder called config. In this new folder, create a file called qalipsis.yml with the following content:
section.programlisting: events: root: warn export: slf4j: enabled: true
section.simpara: This enables the logging of the events with severity WARN and higher to a file called qalipsis-events.log.
Execute your scenario again; the events file is created and contains the details of the individual failed assertions:
section.screen: 2022-03-31T06:33:55.937Z WARN --- step.assertion.failure;io.qalipsis.plugins.netty.tcp.ConnectionAndRequestResult@1e869276 MetersImpl(timeToSuccessfulConnect=null, timeToFailedConnect=null, timeToSuccessfulTlsConnect=null, timeToFailedTlsConnect=null, bytesCountToSend=85, sentBytes=85, timeToFirstByte=PT0.10696475S, timeToLastByte=PT0.1163375S, receivedBytes=394) PT0.10696475S should be <= PT0.1S;campaign=quickstart-campaign-4d39026fd7,minion=quickstart-http-62qr1n87xx,scenario=quickstart,dag=dag-1,step=verify-patch,context-creation=2022-03-31T06:33:55.730Z,parent-step=_629qthims5,_ step-type=Verification,iteration=0,attempts-after-failure=0,isExhausted=true,isTail=true,isCompleted=true_ 2022-03-31T06:33:56.916Z WARN --- step.assertion.failure;io.qalipsis.plugins.netty.tcp.ConnectionAndRequestResult@19a03b37 MetersImpl(timeToSuccessfulConnect=null, timeToFailedConnect=null, timeToSuccessfulTlsConnect=null, timeToFailedTlsConnect=null, bytesCountToSend=85, sentBytes=85, timeToFirstByte=PT0.128673083S, timeToLastByte=PT0.128737375S, receivedBytes=394) PT0.128673083S should be <= PT0.1S;campaign=quickstart-campaign-4d39026fd7,minion=quickstart-http-62sqemkv8j,scenario=quickstart,dag=dag-1,step=verify-patch,context-creation=2022-03-31T06:33:56.735Z,parent-step=_629qthims5,_ step-type=Verification,iteration=0,attempts-after-failure=0,isExhausted=true,isTail=true,isCompleted=true_
section.simpara: Essentially, you want to view the following pertinent parts of the message:
section.screen: 2022-03-31T06:33:55.937Z ... PT0.10696475S should be <= PT0.1S ... campaign=quickstart-campaign-4d39026fd7,... scenario=quickstart,... step=verify-patch

listitem.simpara: The exact instant when the failed assertion occurred:
2022-03-31T06:33:55 (March 31, 2022 at 06:33:55 UTC) in the example above.
The reason for the failure:
The campaign (quickstart-campaign-4d39026fd7), scenario (quickstart-http), and verification step (verify-patch) where the failed assertion occurred.
Discover how to push events to a database, making them easier to view and analyze.

quickstart 1

chapter.title: Overview
chapter.simpara: A scenario specification is the total of all of the steps required to configure the scenario and to execute it at run-time.
In order to provide maximum flexibility for configuring a scenario and the best performance while executing it, QALIPSIS splits its configuration from its execution.

listitem.simpara: In the configuration phase, you develop specifications for the scenario.
In the execution phase, you set the runtime parameters for the scenario(s) you are executing.

chapter.simpara: This section focuses on scenario configuration. Details on scenario execution are provided in .

chapter.title: Configuring a scenario specification
chapter.simpara: A scenario specification is made up of several parts.

listitem.simpara: A call to the Scenario function:
listitem.programlisting: @Scenario fun myScenario()

listitem.simpara: Assigning the scenario a name (hello world):
Configuring the scenario.
Assume the default configuration parameters:
The default parameters include:
minionCount = 1
retryPolicy = no retry ---
Include defined parameters within the configuration, as in the following example:
Identifying the root of the (load-injected) tree receiving all the minions.
Adding step specifications to the tree:

chapter.title: Default scenario configuration
chapter.simpara: Every scenario has a default configuration.

section.title: Number of minions

listitem.simpara: Optional
Default = 1

section.simpara: Whether you want to simulate users, IoT devices or third-party systems, each simulation is identified with a unique minion:

listitem.simpara: The more minions, the more load.
Minions execute concurrently and through all trees.
You can specify a default minionsCount (1000 in the following example):

section.simpara: Load-injected trees receive all of the minions; non-load injected trees receive only one minion.

section.title: Ramping strategies

listitem.simpara: Mandatory
There is no default

section.simpara: Once you have specified the number of minions to be injected into your scenario, you need to specify how they will be injected.
You can use an out-of-the-box strategy or create your own.
In the following sections we will explain how to use a few of the out-of-the-box ramping strategies.

listitem.simpara: Accelerated Ramping Strategies
An accelerating ramping strategy injects either a set number of minions at an accelerated pace or an accelerated number of minions at a set pace.
Accelerated pace using faster.

The following example injects 10 minions to start,then 10 new minions after 2000 ms, 10 new minions after another 1333 (2000 / 1.5) ms, 10 new minions after 888 (2000 / 1.5 / 1.5) ms, continuing until the interval reaches 200 ms.
After the interval reaches 200ms, 10 minions are injected every 200 ms until all minions are active.
The speed-factor at runtime will be applied to the multiplication factor: a speed factor of 2 will reduce the interval time in half.
Accelerated number of minions using more.

The following example injects minions every 200 ms: first 10, then 20 new minions after 200 ms, 40 new minions after another 200 ms, to the limit of 1000 minions every 200ms until all minions are active.
The speed-factor at runtime will be applied to the # of minions injected: a speed factor of 2 will increase the number of minions by 2 at each injection.
regular Ramping Strategy
A regular ramping strategy sets the injection of a set amount of minions at a set pace.
The following example sets the execution of 200 minions every 10 ms.
The speed-factor at runtime will be applied to the interval: a speed factor of 2 will divide the interval by 2.
timeframe Ramping Strategy
A time frame ramping strategy injects an equal number of minions at an equal interval within a set timeframe.
The following example starts an equal number of minions every 20 ms, so that they all are active after 40000 ms.
The speed-factor at runtime will be applied to the interval: a speed factor of 2 will divide the interval by 2.
User Defined Ramping Strategies

A user-defined ramping strategy is totally open to the developer’s creativity or needs. It consists of returning a MinionsStartingLine considering the previous period in milliseconds, the total number of minions to start and the speed factor to apply.
The following example releases 1/100th of all the minions after 1000 ms, then applies different rules depending on the past interval:
If the previous interval was longer than 2 seconds, 100th of all the minions are released after 100ms;
Otherwise, a random interval is calculated applying the speed factor to reduce it, in order to start 10th of all the minions.
Whatever definition you choose:
The maximum number of minions launched cannot be more than the totalmMnions.
Generating a negative interval results in an immediate start.
When calling the first iteration of a user-defined strategy, the initial value of pastPeriodMs is 0.

section.title: Retry policy

listitem.simpara: Optional
Default = no retry

section.simpara: Execution of steps can fail for a variety of reasons, including, but not limited to, an unreachable remote host or invalid data.
When a step execution fails, it may be retried if a retry policy has been set within the scenario configuration or the step configuration.

Once all attempts are executed per the defined retryPolicy, the step execution context is marked as exhausted and only error processing steps are executed. Refer to the section for more information on error processing steps.

chapter.title: Load-injected tree
chapter.simpara: A scenario is a combination of directed acyclic graphs, joined by steps.
There might be several graphs starting concurrently at the scenario root, but only one graph (the specified load-injected branch) is visited by all the minions injected into the scenario.
Other graphs are considered non-injected or "side-trees" and perform data verification for when the branches rejoin.
myScenariostart().doSomething() identifies which graph is the root receiving the load of all the minions at runtime.
myScenario.doSomething() identifies a second graph visited by a unique minion and generally consisting of iterative operations such as polling of data sources.
Note that only one use of .start() is permitted and only one step after it.
To create two load-injected branches, you can use the .split command.
.split provides the output of a unique step to several steps by initiating the divergence of two load-injected branches, both running concurrently and receiving all the minions.
In the following example, two branches are diverging: one with the step .map{} and a second with flatten().filterNotNull(). Both run concurrently after .split.

chapter.title: Singleton step
chapter.simpara: A singleton step is designed to be executed only once by one minion. Its purpose is to gather data to provide to the other minions as they pass through it. The minions can use the data to feed other steps or verify expected results in the tested system.
Examples of a singleton step include:

listitem.simpara: A database poller.
A message consumer.

chapter.simpara: While the singleton step is executed only once, it provides provides data to all the minions passing through it.

A singleton step does not defer from other steps. It is executed using one minion, which looks like any other minion. Even if the singleton step is created on a load-injected branch, it remains aside and is proxied.

The proxy will be executed by all the minions going through the branch, while the singleton step will only have its own one, only aiming at triggering its operation: polling, reading a file, starting a message consumer, etc.
The singleton step and its proxy are connected by a topic, which actually defines the way the polled/read/ consumed records are provided to the minions.

chapter.title: Data distribution from the singleton step to the load minions
chapter.simpara: For convenience purpose, QALIPSIS proposes three different modes of distributing records out of a singleton step:

listitem.simpara: Loop
Unicast/Forward Once
Broadcast

chapter.simpara: Note: You can develop your own singleton step by creating a plugin for it. Details for creating your own plugins will be provided in future updates to this documentation.
QALIPSIS provides all required statements out-of-the-box.

section.title: Loop
section.simpara: When the number of records emitted by the singleton step is finite (such as a file), you might want to loop in the whole set of data to be sure there will always be data for the minions.

informalexample.simpara: Create and insert singleton loop graphic here

section.simpara: All minions receive all the records. When there is no more data for a minion, it receives the same data again from the beginning and loops on the whole set of records as long as it needs new ones.

section.title: Unicast/forward once
section.simpara: Each minion receives the next unused record emitted from the step. Once there are no more records to provide, all the minions remain blocked in the step.

informalexample.simpara: Create and insert singleton-unicast graphic here

section.simpara: In the example, each of the 3 minions receives a record fairly: the first asking is served. However, since the buffer is only 2, the first records are just lost, keeping only a buffer of 2 until the first minion requests a value.

section.title: Broadcast
section.simpara: All the minions receive all the records from the beginning. Once there are no more records to provide, all of the minions, having already received all available records, remain blocked in the step.
If the size of the buffer is limited, minions landing in the step for the first time will not receive everything from the beginning, but only from the size of the buffer.

informalexample.simpara: Create and insert broadcast graphic here

section.simpara: In the example, the all 3 minions receive all the records in the correct order. However, since the buffer is only 2, each newly requesting minion can only receive two records in the past at the earliest.

chapter.title: Defining and configuring a step specification
chapter.simpara: A step specification is defined, depending on the step nature and function, as either:

listitem.simpara: The root of a scenario (the first step after .start()).
or
The successor of a previous step.

chapter.simpara: In the example below, my-tcp is the root step; reuse-tcp is a successor step.
A step specification is configured such that:

listitem.simpara: The step receives the configuration closure {} as a parameter (as in .tcp and .reuse-tcp in the example above).
.configure is called after the step as in the example below:

chapter.title: Step context
chapter.simpara: A step context is the mechanism by which:

listitem.simpara: Data received from the previous step is transported as input to the next steps. The next steps transform the data and provides it as output. The output is forwarded to the next steps as input.
Prior errors are transported through a step.

section.title: Fan-out
section.simpara: To split a branch and add two or more concurrent steps as successors, execute a .split.

In the example below, Anystep receives the output of the previous step as input. Anystep transforms its input per its step configuration. The transformed data is provided as input to both the map and the flatten steps. The map and the flatten steps each do something different with the data.
A graphical representation of the above example is shown below. Note that both branches run concurrently, in no defined execution order.

informalexample.simpara: create and include fan-out distribution graphic here→ more like a flowchart

warning.simpara: If the entity returned by the step providing the data before the .split (Anystep() in the example) is liable to change, do not change it in one branch, as this would have side-effects on the other branch. Consider creating a deep clone of the record that is to be changed to avoid this issue.

section.title: Fan-in/joins
section.simpara: Fan-in/Join functions allow the user to re-join split branches of a scenario or to verify data from different sources.
You can join the branches in one of two ways:

listitem.simpara: Use the name of a concurrent step.
Create a step directly in the join.

section.simpara: The following example uses the name of a concurrent step:
section.programlisting: Anystep { } .innerJoin<EntityWithKey, EntityWithId>( on = { "${it.key}" }, with = "my-datasource-poll-step", // The step `my-datasource-poll-step` is declared in a parallel branch (or earlier in the same branch) and provides an output of type `EntityWithId` with a field `id` being a string in this example. having = { it.id } // Closure to extract the key from the right side, which should match the left side value. ) .map { } // The result is a `Pair<EntityWithKey, EntityWithId>` where string representation of `key` of the `EntityWithKey` equals to the field `id` of the `EntityWithId`.
section.simpara: Records coming from the right side are kept in a cache until one comes from the left side with the same key. Once used, they are evicted from the cache.
When a flow comes from the left side but has no corresponding record yet coming from the right, it waits until the defined timeout on the step. Refer to for more details.
The step context after the join inherits from the one coming from the left. Below is the graphical representation:

informalexample.simpara: insert operators-inner-join graphic here

section.simpara: The following example creates a step directly in the join:
This method is similar to naming a concurrent step except that a step is created in the join. The receiver of the with is the scenario.

section.title: Error processing steps
section.simpara: When an error occurs when executing a step, after all the potential retries, the step context being carried from top to bottom is marked as "exhausted".
From that point of the process, only the steps relevant for error processing are executed, the others are simply bypassed.
The steps relevant for error processing include:

listitem.simpara: catchError: takes the collection of errors as parameter to process them.
catchExhaustedContext: takes the full exhausted step context as parameter, where you can also set the isExhausted flag to false in order execute next steps.

chapter.title: Operators
chapter.simpara: QALIPSIS provides default operators to tweak the execution of your scenario, that do not require the addition of plugins to your classpath.

Out-of-the-box QALIPSIS operators perform a variety of functions including data transformation, data filtering, data verification, altering the behavior of the data, etc. Together with user-defined operators the list of operators is infinite.
Some of the commonly used operators are defined in this section.

section.title: Timing operators
section.simpara: This section provides an explanation and examples of the delay, pace, acceleratingPace, and constantPace timing operators.

listitem.simpara: delay adds a constant delay before executing the next step, keeping the same execution pace as previous step.

informalexample.simpara: insert example and create and insert delay graphic here

listitem.simpara: pace ensures that the next step is executed at an expected pace, whatever the pace of the input is. The calculation applies independently to each minion.
acceleratingPace reduces the waiting interval for each iteration; to slow down the pace, use an accelerator lower than 1.

informalexample.simpara: insert example and create and insert acceleratingPace graphic here

listitem.simpara: constantPace applies a constant pace to execute the successor steps.

informalexample.simpara: insert example and create and insert constantPace graphic here

section.title: Filter operators
section.simpara: This section provides an explanation and examples of the filter, validate, and verify filter operators.

listitem.simpara: filter removes the step contexts having an input not matching the filter specification. Filter is a silent equivalent of validate; it does not generate an error and does not mark the step context as exhausted.

informalexample.simpara: provide an example and insert filter(is even) graphic here

listitem.simpara: validate validates the input. If errors are returned, the step context is marked as exhausted and can then only be processed by error processing steps.

informalexample.simpara: Provide an example and insert validate(is even) graphic here

listitem.simpara: verify validates assertions rather than the input data quality/safety that is validated by validate.

informalexample.simpara: Provide an example and insert verify(assetThat(it).isEven()) graphic here

section.title: Transformation operators
section.simpara: flatThis section provides an explanation and examples of the flatMap, flatten, map, mapWithContext, and onEach transformation operators.

listitem.simpara: flatMap converts a collective input into a single element in the output, with a user-defined strategy.

informalexample.simpara: provide an example and insert flatMap graphic here

listitem.simpara: flatten converts a collective input into a single element in the output for a known collective type (array, iterable, etc.), keeping each item unchanged.

informalexample.simpara: Provide an example and insert flatten graphic here

listitem.simpara: map converts each element into another element in the output.
mapWithContext provides the same capability as map but with the step context as an additional argument.

informalexample.simpara: Provide an example of map and mapWithContext and insert a graphic here

listitem.simpara: onEach executes one or several statements on each element of the input and forwards them unchanged to the output.

informalexample.simpara: Provide an example of onEach and insert a graphic here

section.title: Caching operators
section.simpara: This section provides an explanation and examples of the shelve and unshelve caching operators.

listitem.simpara: shelve caches the provided values in a shared cache.

When using a distributed architecture, make sure the cached value can be serialized by the used implementation of shared state registry. The ID of the minion is automatically added to the key to avoid collision.

informalexample.simpara: Provide an example of shelve and insert a graphic here

listitem.simpara: unshelve fetches one or more records from the shared state registry, previously shelved with the provided keys for the same minion.

informalexample.simpara: Provide an example of unshelve and insert a graphic here

section.title: Flow operators
section.simpara: This section provides an explanation and examples of the pipe, blackHole, and stage flow operators.

listitem.simpara: pipe simply forwards the input to the output.

informalexample.simpara: Provide an example of pipe and insert a graphic here

listitem.simpara: blackHole consumes the input and provides no output.

informalexample.simpara: Provide an example of blackhole and insert a graphic here

listitem.simpara: stage groups a linear graph of steps.
Stages are not mandatory in a scenario, but provide a convenient way to iterate steps. In the example below, after flatten() is completed it repeats 5 times starting at the first map:

informalexample.simpara: insert a stage graphic here if helpful

section.title: Combining operators
section.simpara: The most often used combining operator is collect.
collect collects inputs across all the minions and releases them as a list within the latest received step context.
This operator’s function is the opposite of flatten.

informalexample.simpara: insert a collect example and a graphic here if helpful

chapter.title: Starting QALIPSIS in different modes
chapter.simpara: Two modes of QALIPSIS configuration are currently supported:

listitem.simpara: Interactive
Scenario Selection
Execute QALIPSIS with the option --prompt or -p. as in the following example:
listitem.programlisting: java -jar my-scenario-qalipsis.jar --prompt

listitem.simpara: When prompted, enter the load factor (# of minions), speed factor, ramping strategy, etc.

section.title: Scenario selection
section.simpara: Execute QALIPSIS with the --scenarios or -s as in the following example:
The value is a comma-separated list of an exact value of a scenario, a pattern of the scenario, or both. The pattern may contain wildcards (* or ?) as in the example above.

section.title: Command-line arguments
section.simpara: This section provides an overview and an example of the command-line arguments that are available at runtime.
Note that all command-line arguments can be provided in a file, as explained in .

listitem.simpara: Number of Minions by Scenario.
This command defines how many minions should be used in the load-injected tree. It is not compliant with campaign.minions-factor.
Following is an example of the command to use to set the number of minions:
listitem.programlisting: --- campaign.minions-count-per-scenario (#ofMinions) ---
listitem.simpara: where #ofMinions is an integer and the default value is the minionCount set during configuration of the scenario.
Load Factor.
The load factor command multiplies the number of minions set in the scenario specification by the provided value. It is not compliant with campaign.minions-count-per-scenario.
If the value is set between 0 and 1, the minionCount is reduced.
If the value is set to greater than one (or double,triple, quadruple, etc.) the minionCount is increased.
The default value is always set to 1.
Following is an example of the command to set the load factor:
Speed Factor. The speed factor command is applied on the ramping strategy to accelerate or slow down the pace of the minions released.
For example:
The value for the speed factor is defined the same as for load factor.

section.title: External property sources
section.simpara: QALIPSIS uses Micronaut’s runtime underneath. Hence, you can use its mechanism of Externalized Configuration with Property Sources.
You can overwrite the default configuration and add your own properties by creating a file called qalipsis-config.yml either in the class path or in the working directory or both.
Both the default configuration and the qalipsis-config.yml file can specify the same parameters; however, the qalipsis-config.yml has precedence.

section.title: Injecting values into your scenario
section.simpara: You can inject values into your scenario or its declaring class using the annotation @io.qalipsis.api.annotations.Property as in the following example:

partintro.simpara: QALIPSIS relies on the Dependency injection framework from Micronaut and supports the injection of dependencies in the constructors of the scenario declaring classes and methods annotated with @Scenario.

warning.simpara: Ensure you have the following dependency in the project where your beans or factories are compiled: kapt("io.qalipsis:api-processors:0.4.16-SNAPSHOT")

chapter.title: Injecting a singleton bean
chapter.simpara: You can declare a singleton bean using the annotation @jakarta.inject.Singleton on a type, or use a Micronaut factory.

section.title: Creation of beans
section.simpara: Once beans are created, you can inject them in the constructor or the scenario method:

section.title: Injection of beans

chapter.title: Qualifying the bean to inject
chapter.simpara: When several beans exist for the same type, you might want to select the one to inject. QALIPSIS supports named injection as in the following example.
QALIPSIS only supports name qualifiers; however, you can read more about other qualifiers in the Micronaut documentation.

chapter.title: Injecting a property value
chapter.simpara: Properties for the runtime property sources are also injectable in scenario declaring classes, constructors, and methods.
The default value set in orElse is optional.
More information about property sources is available in the Micronaut documentation.

chapter.title: Injecting iterable containers
chapter.simpara: QALIPSIS supports the injection of a single instance of beans as shown above, as well as an Iterable or any of its subtypes Collection, List, Set and their derivatives and Optional for properties as in the example below.

chapter.title: Summary
chapter.simpara: The sample code below includes all the supported methods of injection for beans and properties.
The example is for a scenario method, but can also be used for the constructor of a class declaring scenarios.

chapter.title: Introduction
chapter.simpara: A plugin is a technical extension designed to extend the core capabilities of QALIPSIS.
Plugins:

listitem.simpara: Tackle the complexity implied by the multiplicity of technologies and use cases to support in the tool.
Reduce the coupling between the technical elements, keeping them safer and easier to maintain.

chapter.simpara: The main advantages of using plugins are:

listitem.simpara: A drastic reduction in the footprint of required libraries to run a scenario.
A better user/developer experience, including:
The ability to focus on what is absolutely relevant to the user/developer.
Having a smaller list of proposals when using auto-completion.
Faster compilation and start times.
Smaller artifacts to store, transport and share.

chapter.simpara: By nature, a plugin is an optional component of QALIPSIS. It allows you to include only what you need while ignoring components and libraries that do not serve your your present concerns.
While a certain number of plugins are proposed by the community to cover the most common use cases and technologies, a plugin can also be an extension only for yourself and remain private.

chapter.title: Plugin functionality
chapter.simpara: A plugin contains one or several technical functionalities of the software that are not part of the core. The most common functionalities that a plugin might implement are:

listitem.simpara: A step to perform an action, such as:
Executing requests on a remote system using a specific protocol.
Polling data from a source.
Transforming data.
An events logger.
A meter registry.

chapter.simpara: Default implementations of the above functionalities are part of the QALIPSIS core. However, the default implementations may not be suited for your scenario (e.g., if productive testing or constant monitoring are requirements). QALIPSIS plugins enhance the above functionalities by providing related implementations.

chapter.title: Plugin integration
chapter.simpara: To create a test scenario, a tiny Gradle project is required. The plugin is simply added as a dependency to the class path of the project. You can read more about scenario configuration within the .
If the development of a scenario requires the plugin to compile (when using a step, for example), the dependency to the plugin artifact must be added to the implementation dependencies.
If the plugin delivers functionalities that you only need at runtime, add it to the runtimeOnly dependencies.

chapter.title: Using QALIPSIS plugins
chapter.simpara: QALIPSIS provides several plugins targeting different technologies, systems and protocols.
To use a plugin, add the related dependency to your project:
Gradle - Groovy DSL:
Gradle - Kotlin DSL:

chapter.title: QALIPSIS available plugins
chapter.simpara: In this section, documentation is provided for currently available plugins that can be used with your QALIPSIS project.
This section will be updated as more plugins are available.
Note: Before using a plugin, confirm that the related dependency has been added to your project:

listitem.simpara: Gradle - Groovy DSL:
Gradle - Kotlin DSL:

entry.simpara: Supports reading a file (JSON, XML or CSV), converting the data to an object, and pushing the object to the next step.
Connects QALIPSIS to a messaging platform that supports JMS (for example, ActiveMQ).
Connects QALIPSIS to an Apache Kafka cluster.
Supports TCP, UDP, HTTP and MQTT clients
Connects connects QALIPSIS to MariaDB, MySQL and PostgreSQL.

section.title: Overview
section.simpara: The cassandra plugin connects QALIPSIS to APACHE Cassandra.

listitem.simpara: Cassandra: APACHE Cassandra
Cassandra Query Language: The Cassandra Query Language (CQL)
DataStax Driver: DataStax.
DataStax: refer to the DataStax.
Datastax is a modern, feature-rich and highly tunable Java client library for Apache Cassandra using Cassandra’s binary protocol and Cassandra Query Language.
poll: polls the newest data periodically.
save: saves records generated from the input and step context.
search: searches records and builds a query from the input and step context.

section.simpara: ===Poll step The poll step within the cassandra plugin polls the newest records from the database table.

listitem.simpara: Scenario or step
The poll step is created only once in a scenario; it is then used throughout the scenario as called. The poll step within the cassandra plugin uses a strategy of “delivered at least once."
connect (required): configures the connection to APACHE Cassandra
keyspace(required): the outermost object that determines how data replicates on nodes.
datacenterProfile(required): an execution profile that encapsulates a group of settings associated with individual queries.
query (required): defines the prepared statement to execute when polling.
parameters (optional): binds values to the clauses of the prepared query.
tieBreaker (required): repeats the first column used for sorting and comparison (⇐ or >=, depending on the ascending or descending order).
pollDelay (required): specifies the delay between query executions.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
broadcast (optional): specifies the broadcast parameters for the step.
Use the poll step with a left join operator to verify the values saved by your tested system in the database.
query requires at least one sorting field (tiebreaker in the example) to filter the newest results and not fetch the same records repeatedly.
The latest known value for tiebreaker is used to filter out values already received from the database.
Refer to the APACHE Cassandra documentation further parameter and configuration information.

section.title: Save step
section.simpara: The save step within the cassandra plugin persists records into a cassandra database using the save query with Cassandra Query Language (CQL).

listitem.simpara: Step
The save step’s input and step context are used to generate documents to save into a table.
connect (required): configures the connection to APACHE Cassandra.
keyspace(required): the outermost object that determines how data replicates on nodes.
datacenterProfile(required): an execution profile that encapsulates a group of settings associated with individual queries.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
Use the save after a collect step in order to reduce the number of queries executed onto the database and reduce the load your scenario generates on it.
The save step is extremely flexible; table, rows, and columns depend on the context and input value received from the previous step.
Refer to the APACHE Cassandra documentation for further parameter and configuration information.

section.title: Search step
section.simpara: The search step within the cassandra plugin searches records in the database using a search query.

listitem.simpara: step
The search step’s input and step context are used to complete the search; the list of results is then forwarded to the next step(s).
connect (required): configures the connection to APACHE Cassandra.
keyspace(required): the outermost object that determines how data replicates on nodes.
datacenterProfile(required): an execution profile that encapsulates a group of settings associated with individual queries.
query (required): defines the prepared statement to execute when searching.
parameters: (optional): binds values to the clauses of the prepared query.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
Use the search step after a collect() step to reduce the number of queries executed onto the database and to reduce the load your scenario generates on the test system.
The query Parameter may contain ordering clauses.
Refer to the APACHE Cassandra documentation for further parameter and configuration information.

section.title: Overview
section.simpara: The elasticsearch plugin connects QALIPSIS to Elasticsearch.

listitem.simpara: Elasticsearch: https://www.elastic.co/
JAVA API: refer to Elasticsearch JAVA Client.
poll: polls the newest data periodically.
save: saves a batch records generated from the input and step context.
search: searches records and builds a query from the input and step context.

section.title: Poll step
section.simpara: The poll step within the elasticsearch plugin periodically polls data from Elasticsearch. The output is a list of ElasticsearchDocument containing JSON data or deserialized to objects.

listitem.simpara: Scenario

The poll step is created only once in a scenario; it is then used throughout the scenario as called. The poll step within the elasticsearch plugin uses a strategy of “delivered exactly once”. The output of the poll step is an ElasticsearchDocument that contains maps of column to values.
index: sets the elasticsearch indices to use for the queries; defaults to all.
client: configures the REST client to connect to Elasticsearch.
query: configures the builder for the JSON query to perform the first poll.
queryParameters(optional): adds parameters to the query.
mapper: configures the JSON Mapper to deserialize the documents.
tieBreaker (required): repeats the first column used for sorting and comparison (⇐ or >=, depending on the ascending or descending order).
pollDelay (required): specifies the delay between query executions.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
broadcast (optional): specifies the broadcast parameters for the step.
The poll step is generally used in conjunction with a join to assert data or inject data into a workflow.
The query parameter requires at least one sorting field (tiebreaker in the example) to filter the newest results and not fetch the same records repeatedly.
The latest known value for tiebreaker is used to filter out values already received from the database.

The output of the poll step is an ElasticsearchDocument that contains maps of column names to values. If flatten is called, the records are provided one by one to the next step; otherwise each poll batch remains complete.
Refer to Elasticsearch Query Parameters for more information on query parameters.
Refer to the Elasticsearch documentation for further parameter and configuration information.

section.title: Save step
section.simpara: The save step within the elasticsearch plugin persists a batch of documents into Elasticsearch.

listitem.simpara: Step
The save step’s input and step context are used to generate a list of documents that are forwarded to Elasticsearch.
documentsFactory: defines the strategy to create documents to save from the input and step context.
client: configures the REST client to connect to Elasticsearch.
mapper: configures the JSON Mapper to serialize the documents.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
Refer to the Elasticsearch documentation for further information on parameter configuration.

section.title: Search step
section.simpara: on The search step within the elasticsearch plugin searches documents in the Elasticsearch database using a search query.

listitem.simpara: Step
The search step’s input and step context are used to generate a query; the batch of results is then forwarded to the next step(s).
client: configures the REST client to connect to Elasticsearch.
query: configures the builder for the JSON query to perform a search.
index: sets the elasticsearch indices to use for the queries; defaults to all.
queryParameters(optional): adds parameters to the query.
mapper configures the JSON Mapper to deserialize the documents.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
fetchAll: specifies that all the documents have to be fetched, even over the result-sized window.

fetchAll must be combined with either a scroll activation or a sorting definition in the query. Refer to the current; The latter case uses search after to page all the data. New data coming between two requests will be fetched. The requests stops when the number of fetched documents reaches the total hits of the latest response. Note that this could lead to an infinite step execution if data are added to the results set in elasticsearch on a regular basis.
Refer to the Elasticsearch documentation for further information on parameter configuration.
For specifics on queryParameters refer to Elasticsearch Query Parameters.
For specifics on search parameters, refer to Elasticsearch Search Parameters

chapter.title: graphite plugin
chapter.simpara: The graphite_ plugin connects or supports…​

section.title: Overview
section.simpara: The influxdb plugin connects QALIPSIS to InfluxDB.

listitem.simpara: InfluxDB: https://www.influxdata.com/
Flux: https://docs.influxdata.com/flux/v0.x/
influxdb-client-kotlin: refer to the influxdb-client-kotlin.
poll: polls the newest data periodically.
save: saves a batch of records generated from the input and step context.
search: searches records and builds a query from the input and step context.

section.title: Poll step
section.simpara: The poll step within the influxdb() plugin polls the newest records from the database, with a delay between each execution of a select query.

listitem.simpara: Scenario
The poll step is created only once in a scenario; it is then used throughout the scenario as called. The poll step within the influxdb plugin uses a strategy of “delivered at least once”.
connect (required): configures the connection to InfluxDB.
query (required): specifies a query respecting the requirements of Flux v.2.0.
tieBreaker (required): repeats the first field used for sorting and comparison (⇐ or >=, depending on the ascending or descending order).
parameters (optional): binds values to the clauses of the prepared query.
pollDelay (required): specifies the delay between query executions.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
broadcast (optional): specifies the broadcast parameters for the step.
Use the poll step with a join operator to verify the values that the tested system saves to the database.
The token can be created automatically using your password and username.
query requires at least one sorting field (tiebreaker in the example) to filter the newest results and not fetch the same records repeatedly.
The latest known value for tiebreaker filters out the values already received directly from the database.
Refer to the InfluxDB documentation for further parameter and configuration information.

section.title: Save step
section.simpara: The save step within the influxdb() plugin persists a batch of records into the influxdb bucket using a specific points class.

listitem.simpara: Step
The save step’s input and step context are used to generate the bucket, the list of points with their measurement, and tags and fields, which are then combined and forwarded to the database.
connect (required): configures the connection to InfluxDB.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
Use the save step after a collect step to reduce the number of queries executed onto the database and to reduce the load your scenario generates on the test system.
The step is extremely flexible: bucket, tags, measurement and fields might depend on the context and input value received from the previous step.
token can be created automatically using your password and username.
Refer to the InfluxDB documentation for further parameter and configuration information.

section.title: Search step
section.simpara: The search step within the influxdb plugin searches records in the database using a search query.

listitem.simpara: Step
The search step’s input and step context are used to generate a query; the batch of results is then forwarded to the next step(s).
search: generates the search query from the step input and context.
connect (required): configures the connection to InfluxDB.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
Use the search step after a collect step to reduce the number of queries executed onto the database and to reduce the load the scenario generates on the test system.
token can be created automatically using your password and username.
Refer to the InfluxDB documentation for further parameter and configuration information.

section.title: Overview
section.simpara: The jackson plugin supports reading a (JSON, XML or CSV) file, converting the data to an object, and pushing the object to the next step.

listitem.simpara: JsonMapper: refer to FasterXML.
csvToMap and csvToObject: reads a csv file, and converts the data to a Map (or an Object).
jsonToMap and jsonToObject: reads a json file and converts the data to a Map (or Object).
xmlToObject: reads an xml file and converts the data to an object.

section.title: csvToMap and csvToObject
section.simpara: The csvToMap and csvToObject steps within the jackson plugin read a CSV resource (file, classpath resource, URL) and returns each item as an instance of an Object or Map.

listitem.simpara: Step

The csvToMap and csvToObject step specification has a targetClass property to which the output lines should be mapped. The step reads a CSV file, converts the data to a map (or an object) and pushes the map (or object) to the next step.
classpath(file): reads the data from a specified class path resource; file is the path to the resource with the leading slash ignored.
file(path): reads the data from a specified plain file in the file system; path is the path to the file, either absolute or relative to the current working directory.
url(url): specifies and links to the url of the data resource.
mapper: configures the JSON Mapper to deserialize the records.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
Only one path (classpath(file), file(path), or url(url)) to the resource is allowed for each csvToObject or csvToMap step.
Refer to the jackson documentation for further congiguration and parameter information.

section.title: xmlToObject
section.simpara: The xmlToObject step within the jackson plugin reads an XML resource (classpath, file, or url) and returns each item as an instance of an object of the specified class.

listitem.simpara: Step

The xmlToObject step specification has a targetClass property to which the output lines should be mapped. The step reads an XML file, converts the data to an Object, and pushes the Object to the next step.
classpath(file): reads the data from a specified class path resource; file is the path to the resource with the leading slash ignored.
file(path): reads the data from a specified plain file in the file system; path is the path to the file, either absolute or relative to the current working directory.
url(url): specifies and links to the url of the data resource.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
Only one path (classpath(file), file(path), or url(url)) to the resource is allowed for each xmlToObject step.
Refer to the jackson documentation for further congiguration and parameter information.

section.title: jsonToMap and jsonToObject
section.simpara: The jsonToMap and jsonToObject steps within the jackson plugin read a JSON resource (file, classpath resource, URL) and returns each item as an instance of map or object).

listitem.simpara: Step

The jsonToMap and jsonToObject step specification has a targetClass property to which the output lines should be mapped. The step reads a JSON file, converts the data to a Map (or an Object) and pushes the Map (or Object) to the next step.
classpath(file): reads the data from a specified path resource; file is the path to the resource with the leading slash ignored.
file(path): reads the data from a specified plain file in the file system; path is the path to the file, either absolute or relative to the current working directory.
url(url): specifies and links to the `url of the data resource.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
Only one path (classpath(file), file(path), or url(url)) to the resource is allowed for each jsnToObject or jsnToMap step.
Refer to the jackson documentation for further configuration and parameter information.

section.title: Overview
section.simpara: The jms plugin connects QALIPSIS to a messaging platform that supports JMS.

listitem.simpara: JMS: https://www.oracle.com/java/technologies/java-message-service.html
jms()
JMS API: refer to Java Interface Summary.
consume: consumes data from a messaging platform supporting JMS.
produce: pushes data to a messaging platform supporting JMS.

section.title: Consume step
section.simpara: The consume step within the jms plugin consumes records from ActiveMQ.

listitem.simpara: Scenario
The consume step polls data from the designated ActiveMQ connection, step context, and the data deserializer.
consume: configures the the ActiveMQ connection.
queues: specifies the queue(s) for the connection.
QueueConnection: provides the java ActiveMQ connection for the specified queues.
topics: specifies the topic(s) for the connection.
TopicConnection: provides the java ActiveMQ connection for the specified topics.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
broadcast (optional): specifies the broadcast parameters for the step.
deserialize: specifies the class to use to deserialize the body of the response.
The ActiveMQ connection can be configured for either queues (QeueConnection) or topics (TopicConnection), but not both.
Refer to the JMS documentation for for further parameter and configuration information.

section.title: Produce step
section.simpara: The produce step within the jms plugin produces records in ActiveMQ using JMS.

listitem.simpara: Step
The produce step produces a batch of records into one or more JMS destinations. The record values may be provided by previous steps.
records: generates and configures a list of produced records.
destination: defines the destination for each JmsProducerRecord as either queues (QueueConnection) or topics (TopicConnection).
messageType: indicates the JmsMessageType as: TEXT, AUTO, BYTES, or OBJECT.
value: defines the message to be displayed at the destination.
connect: specifies the connection type for the JmsProducerRecord(s).
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
If you are not sure about the JmsMessageType, use AUTO to allow the system to infer the type and display the result.
Refer to the JMS documentation for further parameter and configuration information.

section.title: Overview
section.simpara: The kafka plugin connects QALIPSIS to an APACHE Kafka cluster.

listitem.simpara: Kafka: https://kafka.apache.org/
Kafka: refer to Apache Kafka
consume: consumes data from topics of an Apache Kafka cluster.
produce: pushes data to topics of the Apache Kafka cluster.

section.title: Consume step
section.simpara: The consume step within the kafka plugin polls data from topics of an Apache Kafka cluster and forwards the data to the next step, either in batches or individually.
This step is generally used in conjunction with a left join to assert data or inject data into a workflow.
The consume step supports multithreading (concurrent consumers) using the concurrency property.

listitem.simpara: Scenario

The consume step starts a polling loop for the designated client, using the provided subscription topics name, or topicsPattern and groupId. When new records appear, consume polls them and sends them to the next step(s).

listitem.programlisting: kafka().consume { name = "kafka-consumer" bootstrap(bootstrap) topics(testProducerTopic) groupId("kafka-left") pollTimeout(100) offsetReset(OffsetResetStrategy.EARLIEST) }

listitem.simpara: bootstrap: configures the bootstrap hosts of the Kafka cluster; defaults to localhost:9092.
groupId: defines the consumer group.
pollTimeout defines the consumer poll timeout to wait for records; defaults to 1 second.
offsetReset (required): defines the strategy to apply when the consumer starts consuming messages from the queue.
maxPolledRecords: defines the maximum number of records returned in a unique poll batch; defaults to 500.
pollDelay (required): specifies the delay between query executions.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
broadcast (optional): specifies the broadcast parameters for the step.
Use the consume step with a left join to assert data or inject data into a workflow.
Valid offsetReset strategies are:
OffsetResetStrategy.EARLIEST: starts consuming messages from the beginning of the queue.
OffsetResetStrategy.LATEST: starts consuming messages when the consumer starts, ignoring all previous messages in the queue.
OffsetResetStrategy.LATEST is the default.
Refer to the APACHE KAFKA documentation for further parameter and configuration information.

section.title: Produce step
section.simpara: The produce step within the kafka plugin pushes data onto topics of the Kafka broker and forwards the data as input to the next step.

listitem.simpara: Scenario
The produce step’s input and step context are used to generate values to complete the step. The batch of results, or a single result, is then forwarded to the next step(s).
Serdes.ByteArray().serializer(): describes how to serialize the record keys to send to kafka.
Serdes.String().serializer(): describes how to serialize the record values to send to kafka.
bootstrap configures the bootstrap hosts of the Kafka cluster; defaults to localhost:9092.
clientName defines the name of the kafka client
records (required): generates and configures a list of produced records.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
Refer to the APACHE KAFKA documentation for further parameter and configuration information.

section.title: Overview
section.simpara: The mongodb plugin connects QALIPSIS to MongoDb.

listitem.simpara: MongoDB: https://mongodb.com/
MongoDB Reactive Streams Driver: refer to MongoDB Java Reactive Streams.
poll: polls the newest data periodically.
save: saves a batch of documents generated from the input and step context.
search: searches documents and builds a query from the input and step context.

section.title: Poll step
section.simpara: The poll step within the mongodb plugin polls the newest records from a database table, with a delay between each execution.

listitem.simpara: Scenario
The poll step is created only once in a scenario; it is then used throughout the scenario as called. The poll step within the mongodb plugin uses a strategy of “delivered at least once”.
connect (required): configures the connection to MongoDb.
query (required): specifies a document respecting the requirements of the target database.
tieBreaker (required): repeats the first column used for sorting and comparison (⇐ or >=, depending on the ascending or descending order).
parameters (optional): binds values to the clauses of the prepared query.
pollDelay (required): specifies the delay between query executions.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
broadcast (optional): specifies the broadcast parameters for the step.
Use the poll step with a join operator if you need to verify the values saved by your tested system in the database.
The query parameter requires at least one sorting field (tiebreaker in the example) to filter the newest results and not fetch the same records repeatedly.
The latest known value for tiebreaker is used to filter out the values already received from the database.
Refer to the Mongodb documentation for further parameter and configuration information.

section.title: Save step
section.simpara: The save step within the 'mongodb` plugin persists a batch of documents into MongoDB.

listitem.simpara: Step
The save step’s input and step context are used to generate the table, the list of columns and their values, which are then combined and forwarded to the database.
connect (required): configures the connection to MongoDb.
query (required): specifies a document respecting the requirements of the target database.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
Use the save step after a collect step in order to reduce the number of queries executed onto the database and to reduce the load your scenario generates on the test system.
The save step is extremely flexible; database, collection and documents may depend on the context and input value received from the previous step.
Refer to the Mongodb documentation for further parameter and configuration information.

section.title: Search step
section.simpara: The search step within the mongodb plugin searches records in the database using a search query.

listitem.simpara: Step
The search step’s input and step context are used to generate a document and the values to complete it; the batch of results is then forwarded to the next step(s).
connect (required): configures the connection to MongoDb.
search (required): defines the search parameters including database, collection query and sort.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
Use this step after a collect() step in order to reduce the number of queries executed onto the database and to reduce the load your scenario generates on the test system.
Refer to the Mongodb documentation for further parameter and configuration information.

section.title: Overview
section.simpara: The netty plugin supports TCP, UDP, HTTP and MQTT clients.

listitem.simpara: HTTP/1.1: https://httpwg.org/
HTTP/2: https://httpwg.org/
MQTT: https://mqtt.org/
TCP (Transmission Control Protocol): https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc793
UDP (User Datagram Protocol): https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc768
Netty: refer to the Netty project.
HTTP: executes an HTTP request using HTTP 1.1 or HTTP 2.0.
HTTP WITH: executes an HTTP request reusing the HTTP connection created by a precedent step.
CLOSE HTTP: closes an HTTP connection created by a precedent step.
MQTT Publish: sends data to an MQTT server.
MQTT Subscribe: consumes data from an MQTT server.
TCP: exchanges data with a TCP server.
TCP WITH: exchanges data reusing the TCP connection created by a precedent step.
CLOSE TCP: closes a TCP connection created by a precedent step.
UDP: exchanges data with a UDP endpoint.

section.title: HTTP step
section.simpara: The HTTP step within the netty plugin executes an HTTP request using HTTP 1.1 or HTTP 2.0.

listitem.simpara: Scenario and Step
The HTTP step establishes a connection with the server using either a pool of connections or an individual connection for each minion.
If an individual connection is used, the connection is maintained until the latest execution for the minion is performed, including the ones from the related HTTP WITH step, as in the example.
When the step is kept open without limit, a CLOSE TCP step must be executed to properly close the connections and avoid saturation of the system.

Be aware that the number of open connections that can be concurrently maintained depends on the configuration of your operating system. If there are issues with maintaining open connections, try adapting the limits of the file descriptors.
request: a factory to create the request to exchange, using the step context and the input as arguments.
connect: configures the connection to the remote endpoint.
version: version of the HTTP protocol to use: HTTP_1_1 or HTTP_2_0,; defaults to HTTP_1_1.
url: the root URL, the path of the request is appended to it.
connectTimeout: limit duration to fully establish the connection; defaults to 10 seconds.
readTimeout: limit duration to receive a response and read it totally after a request; defaults to 10 seconds.
shutdownTimeout: limit duration to close a connection; defaults to 10 seconds.
sendBufferSize: the size of the buffer used to prepare the data to send; defaults to 1024 bytes.
receiveBufferSize: the size of the buffer to receive the data of the response; defaults to 1024 bytes.
nettyChannelOptions: overwrites the options of the netty channel, use with care.
noDelay: disables the Nagle Algorithm; defaults to true.
keepConnectionAlive: when set to true, the HTTP header Connection is set to Keep-Alive and the client connection is kept open until CLOSEHTTP() is called.
tls: configuration for SSL/TLS
disableCertificationVerification: set it to true when the certificate on server should not be verified (for example for a self-signed certificate)
protocols: supported protocols; defaults to the netty default for the related HTTP version.
ciphers: supported cipher algorithms; defaults to the netty` default for the related HTTP version.
proxy: configuration to exchange HTTP data via a proxy; defaults to no configuration.
address: the host and port of the proxy.
type: type of the proxy to use: HTTP, SOCKS5; defaults to HTTP.
authenticate: username and optional password to authenticate to the proxy, if it supports and requires it.
charset: character encoding of the HTTP requests,
maxContentLength: maximal expected size of a request, in bytes, defaults to 1048576, which is the minimum allowed.
inflate: enables the acceptance of compressed response. Compression of the request is not supported.

followRedirections: when the server returns an HTTP status 3xx (redirection) in the response, the client will resend the request to the provided location. Otherwise, the response is kept as is. The argument maxRedirections provides the maximal number of redirections to follow; defaults to 10. By default, the redirections are not followed at all. Note that following a redirections makes the request to be sent there, exactly as it is (same method and content).
pool: configuration of the pool of connections; the pool is disabled by default.
size: number of constant connections maintained in the pool.
checkHealthBeforeUse: verifies the health of the connection when polled from the pool and before it is used.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
deserialize: specifies the class to use to deserialize the body of the response.
Refer to the Netty documentation for further configuration and parameter information.

section.title: HTTP WITH step
section.simpara: The HTTP WITH step within the netty plugin executes an HTTP request reusing the HTTP connection created by a precedent step.

listitem.simpara: Step

The HTTP WITH step is a direct or indirect follower of an HTTP step. HTTP WITH specifies the name of the step that owns the connection, and reuses a connection from that step as it was created (either from a pool or the connection attached to the minion).
HTTP WITH cannot configure the connection differently than the HTTP step it relates to.
netty().httpWith("name-of-a-previous-HTTP-step"): the name of the HTTP step that owns the connection.
request: a factory to create the request to exchange, using the step context and the input as arguments.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
deserialize: specifies the class to use to deserialize the body of the response.
Refer to the Netty documentation for further configuration and parameter information.

section.title: CLOSE HTTP step
section.simpara: The CLOSE HTTP step within the netty plugin closes the HTTP connection attached to the minion, and created by a direct or indirect ancestor step, when the tail of the minion workflow is received.
It is always a best practice to use a CLOSE HTTP step when reusing an HTTP connection across several steps without a pool of connections.

listitem.simpara: Step
The CLOSE HTTP step is a direct or indirect follower of an HTTP step which keeps the connection open. CLOSE HTTP simply forwards the input it receives to the next step(s).
closeHttp (required) specifies the name of an HTTP step owning the HTTP connection to close.
