monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
Refer to the Netty documentation for further configuration and parameter information.

section.title: MQTT publish step
section.simpara: The MQTT Publish step within the netty plugin sends data to an MQTT server.

listitem.simpara: To be provided.
To be provided.

listitem.programlisting: To be provided.

listitem.simpara: To be provided.
To be provided.
The MQTT Subscribe Step within the netty plugin consumes data from an MQTT server.
To be provided.
To be provided.

listitem.programlisting: To be provided.

listitem.simpara: To be provided.
To be provided

section.title: TCP step
section.simpara: The TCP step within the netty plugin is a client for TCP.

listitem.simpara: Scenario and Step
The TCP step establishes a connection with the server using either a pool of connections or an individual connection for each minion.
If an individual connection is used, the connection is maintained until the latest execution for the minion is performed, including the ones from the related TCP WITH step, as in the example.
When the step is kept open without limit, a CLOSE TCP step should be executed to properly close the connections and avoid a saturation of the system.

Be aware that the number of open connections that can be concurrently maintained depends on the configuration of your operating system. If there are issues with maintaining open connections, adapting the limits for file descriptors may help.
request: a factory to create the request to exchange, using the step context and the input as arguments; the request is an array of bytes.
connect:configures the connection to the remote endpoint.
address:hostname or IP address and port of the remote server.
connectTimeout: limit duration to fully establish the connection; defaults to 10 seconds.
readTimeout:limit duration to receive and read a response after a request; defaults to 10 seconds.
shutdownTimeout:limit duration to close a connection; defaults to 10 seconds.
sendBufferSize: the size of the buffer used to prepare the data to send; defaults to 1024 bytes.
receiveBufferSize: the size of the buffer to receive the data of the response; defaults to 1024 bytes.
nettyChannelOptions: overwrites the options of the netty channel, use with care.
noDelay: disables the Nagle Algorithm; defaults to true.
tls: configuration for SSL/TLS
disableCertificationVerification: set to true if the certificate on the server should not be verified (for example for a self-signed certificate).
protocols: supported protocols; defaults to the netty default.
ciphers: supported cipher algorithms; defaults to the netty default.
proxy: configuration to exchange data via a proxy; defaults to no configuration.
address: hostname or IP address and port of the proxy server.
type: type of the proxy to use: SOCKS4, SOCKS5; defaults to SOCKS4.
authenticate: username and optional password to authenticate to the proxy, if the proxy supports and requires it.
pool: configuration of the pool of connections; defaults to disabled.
size: number of constant connections maintained in the pool.
checkHealthBeforeUse: verifies the health of the connection when polled from the pool and before it is used.
Refer to the Netty documentation for further configuration and parameter information.

section.title: TCP WITH step
section.simpara: The TCP WITH step within the netty plugin sends a TCP payload, reusing the connection created by a precedent step.

listitem.simpara: Step

The TCP WITH step is a direct or indirect follower of a TCP step. TCP WITH specifies the name of the step that owns the connection, and reuses a connection from that step as it was created (either from a pool or the connection attached to the minion).
TCP WITH cannot configure the connection differently than the TCP step it relates to.
request: a factory to create the request to exchange, using the step context and the input as arguments.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
Refer to the Netty documentation for further configuration and parameter information.

section.title: CLOSE TCP step
section.simpara: The CLOSE TCP step within the netty plugin closes the TCP connection, attached to a minion and created by a direct or indirect ancestor step, when the tail of the minion workflow is received.
It is best practice to use a CLOSE TCP step when reusing a TCP connection across several steps without a pool of connections.

listitem.simpara: Step
The CLOSE TCP step is a direct or indirect follower of an HTTP step which keeps the connection open. CLOSE TCP simply forwards the input it receives to the next step(s).

listitem.programlisting: netty().closeHttp("name-of-a-previous-HTTP-step")

listitem.simpara: closeHttp (required): specifies the name of the HTTP step to close.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
Refer to the Netty documentation for further configuration and parameter information.

section.title: UDP step
section.simpara: The UDP step within the netty plugin is a client for UDP.
It is best practice to use the UDP step when reusing a TCP connection across several steps without a pool of connections.
Ancestors: Scenario and Step

listitem.simpara: The UDP step creates a UDP Datagram packet from the request and sends it to the remote endpoint and expects a response.
request: a factory to create the request to exchange, using the step context and the input as arguments, the request is an array of bytes
connect: configures the connection to the remote endpoint
address: hostname or IP address and port of the remote endpoint
connectTimeout: limit duration to fully establish the connection; defaults to 10 seconds.
readTimeout: limit duration to receive a response and read it totally after a request; defaults to 10 seconds.
shutdownTimeout: limit duration to close a connection; defaults to 10 seconds.
sendBufferSize: the size of the buffer used to prepare the data to send; defaults to 1024 bytes.
receiveBufferSize: the size of the buffer to receive the data of the response; defaults to 1024 bytes.
nettyChannelOptions: overwrites the options of the netty channel, use with care.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
Refer to the Netty documentation for further configuration and parameter information.

section.title: Overview
section.simpara: The r2dbc-jasync plugin connects QALIPSIS to MariaDB, MySQL and PostgreSQL.

listitem.simpara: MariaDB: https://mariadb.org/
MySQL: https://www.mysql.com
PostgreSQL: https://www.postgresql.org/
Jasync-sql: refer to jasync-sql
Jasync-sql is an asynchronous database driver for PostgreSQL and MySQL written in kotlin.
poll: polls the newest data periodically.
save: saves a batch of records generated from the input and step context.
search: searches records and builds a query from the input and step context.

section.title: Poll step
section.simpara: The poll step within the r2bc-jasync plugin polls the newest records from the database, respecting a delay between each execution of a select query.

listitem.simpara: Scenario
The poll step is created only once in a scenario; it is then used throughout the scenario as called. The poll step within the r2b-jaysync plugin uses a strategy of “delivered at least once”.
connection (required): specifies the connection parameters.
query (required): specifies a query respecting the requirements of the target database (MariaDB, MySQL, PostgreSQL,).
parameters (optional): binds values to the clauses of the prepared query.

pollDelay (required): specifies the delay between query executions. tieBreaker (required): repeats the first column used for sorting and comparison ( ⇐ or >=, depending on the ascending or descending order).
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
broadcast (optional): specifies the broadcast parameters for the step.
Use the poll step with a join operator when you need to verify the values saved by your tested system in the database.
query requires at least one sorting field (known as tiebreaker in the example) to filter the newest results and not fetch the same records repeatedly.
The latest known value for the tiebreaker is used to filter out the values already received directly from the database.
Refer to Configuring and Managing Connections for further parameter and configuration information.

section.title: Save step
section.simpara: The save step within the r2bc-jasync plugin persists a batch of records into an SQL table using a prepared statement.

listitem.simpara: Step
The save step’s input and step context are used to generate the table and the list of columns and their values, which are then combined and forwarded to the database.
connection (required): specifies the connection parameters.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
Use the save step after a collect() step in order to reduce the number of queries executed onto the database and to reduce the load your scenario generates on the test system.
The save step is extremely flexible; table, columns and values might depend on the context and input value received from the previous step
Refer to Configuring and Managing Connections for further parameter and configuration information.

section.title: Search step
section.simpara: The search step within the r2bc-jasync plugin searches records in the database using a search query.

listitem.simpara: Step
The search step’s input and step context are used to generate a prepared statement and the values to complete it; the batch of results is then forwarded to the next step(s).
connection (required): specifies the connection parameters.
query: generates a prepared statement from the step’s context and input.
parameters (optional): binds values to the clauses of the prepared query.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
Use the search step after a collect step in order to reduce the number of queries executed onto the database and to reduce the load your scenario generates on the test system.
Refer to Configuring and Managing Connections for further parameter and configuration information.

section.title: Overview
section.simpara: The rabbitmq plugin connects QALIPSIS to the RabbitMQ broker.

listitem.simpara: RabbitMQ: https://www.rabbitmq.com/
RabbitMQ: refer to rabbitMQ
consume: polls data from the rabbitmq broker.
produce: pushes data to the rabbitmq broker.

section.title: Consume step
section.simpara: The consume step within the rabbitmq plugin consumes data from queues of the rabbitmq broker and forwards the data to the next step.
This step is generally used in conjunction with join to assert data or inject data into a workflow.

This step supports multithreading concurrent consumers using the concurrency() property. When the concurrency is set to more than 1, the sequence of the records transmitted to the next step in no longer guaranteed.
To learn more, visit the RabbitMQ website.

listitem.simpara: Scenario
The consume step starts a polling loop for consumption of the designated queue. As new records appear, consume polls them and sends them to the next step.
queue: defines the name of the queue to consume.
connection: configures the connection to the RabbitMQ broker; defaults to localhost:5672
concurrency: defines the number of concurrent channels producing messages to RabbitMQ; defaults to 1.
prefetchCount: defines the prefetch count value; defaults to 10.
pollDelay (required): specifies the delay between query executions.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
broadcast (optional): specifies the broadcast parameters for the step.
Use the consume step with join to assert data or inject data into a workflow.
Learn more about prefetchCount by reading the RabbitMQ Prefetch documentation.
To configure the consume step, consult the rabbitMQ documentation.

section.title: Produce step
section.simpara: The produce step within the rabbitmq plugin provides messages to the rabbitmq broker using a query; the messages are then forwarded as input to the next step.

listitem.simpara: Scenario
The produce step’s input and step context are used to generate the values to complete the step; results are then forwarded to the next step(s).
concurrency defines the number of concurrent channels producing messages to rabbitmq; defaults to 1.
connection: configures the connection to the rabbitmq broker; defaults to localhost:5672.
records: configures and generates a list of produced records.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
For more information on configuring the connection to rabbitmq, refer to Connecting to RabbitMQ.
For more information on configuring the produce step, refer to the rabbitMQ documentation.

chapter.title: redis-lettuce plugin
chapter.simpara: The redis-lettuce plugin connects QALIPSIS to Redis.

listitem.simpara: Redis: https://redis.io/
Lettuce: refer to Lettuce.
poll: polls the newest data periodically.
save: saves a batch of records generated from the input and step context.
consume: consumes data from redis streams.
produce: pushes messages to redis streams.

section.title: Poll step
section.simpara: The poll step within the redis-lettuce plugin polls the newest records from a redis key, with a delay between each execution of polling.

listitem.simpara: Scenario
The poll step is created only once in a scenario; it is then used throughout the scenario as called.
The KeyOrPattern to be used in the poll is set to one of the following valid values: scan, sscan, hscan, zscan.
The native redis-lettuce command is called using the KeyOrPattern provided.
Connection(required): specifies the connection parameters.
redisConnectionType (required): indicates the connection type as SINGLE, CLUSTER or SENTINEL.
KeyOrPattern (required): specifies the key or pattern that will be polled after each pollDelay.
pollDelay (required): specifies the delay between query executions.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
broadcast (optional): specifies the broadcast parameters for the step.
Use the poll step with a join operator when you need to verify the values saved by your tested system in the database.
In addition to HSCAN (polllHscan in the example), the poll step also supports the following redis commands: SCAN (pollScan), SSCAN (pollSscan), and ZSCAN (pollZscan).
If redisConnectionType is set to SENTINEL, the parameter masterId is necessary. For more information, refer to: Redis discovery using Redis Sentinel.
Refer to the Redis documentation for further parameter and configuration information.

section.title: Save step
section.simpara: The save step within the redis-lettuce plugin persists a batch of records into redis.

listitem.simpara: Step

The save step’s input and step context are used to generate a valid redis command and the values to complete it; the batch of results is then forwarded to the next step(s). The records are saved individually.
Connection(required): specifies the connection parameters.
redisConnectionType (required): indicates the connection type as SINGLE, CLUSTER or SENTINEL.
records (required): generates and configures a list of saved records.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
The save step supports the following redis commands: SET, SADD, HSET, ZADD. The provided command may depend on the context and input value received from the previous step.
If the redisConnectionType is set to SENTINEL, the parameter masterId is necessary. For more information, refer to Redis discovery using Redis Sentinel.
Refer to the Redis documentation for further parameter and configuration information.

section.title: Consume step
section.simpara: The consume step within the redis-lettuce plugin records data from redis streams.

listitem.simpara: Scenario

The consume step creates a consumer group in a redis stream with the provided connection, consumer group name, and concurrency. If concurrency is set to 1, records are transmitted sequentially to the next step.
connection(required): configures the connection to Qalipsis.
redisConnectionType (required): indicates the connection type as SINGLE, CLUSTER or SENTINEL. The default is SINGLE.
concurrency(required): configures the level of coroutines consuming the Redis streams.
offset(required): sets the offset value to FROM_BEGINNING, LAST_CONSUMED or LATEST for each topic and consumer group.
broadcast (optional): specifies the broadcast parameters for the step.
pollDelay (required): specifies the delay between query executions.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
If redisConnectionType is set to SENTINEL, the parameter masterId is necessary. For more information, refer to: Redis discovery using Redis Sentinel.
If concurrency is set to more than 1, the records transmitted to the next step may not be in sequence.
Refer to the Redis documentation for further parameter and configuration information.

section.title: Produce step
section.simpara: The produce step within the redis-lettuce plugin produces records into the Redis streams.

listitem.simpara: Step
The produce step produces a batch of records into one or more redis stream topics. The record values may be provided by previous step(s).
redisConnectionType (required): sets the connection type as SINGLE, CLUSTER or SENTINEL.
records (required): generates and configures a list of produced records.
monitoring(optional): configures the monitoring parameters for the step.
If redisConnectionType is set to SENTINEL, the parameter masterId is necessary. For more information, refer to: Redis discovery using Redis Sentinel.
Refer to the Redis documentation for further parameter and configuration information.

chapter.title: Overview
chapter.simpara: QALIPSIS aims at giving insights into the behavior of tested systems from a performance perspective.
The QALIPSIS user-interdace provides the tools to create, run, monitor and view results of test campaigns from your browser.
In this section, you will find infornation on topics related to the QALIPSIS User interface.

listitem.simpara: Navigate to the Qualipsis Welcome page (https://alpha.qalipsis.io)
Enter your Username and Password.
Select the tenant to use from the drop down box.
Click on 'Submit' to log into QALIPSIS.
If a Tenant selection message is not displayed, you will be logged in to QALIPSIS with the tenant you were using in your most recent login.
You can can select a different tenant at any time once you are logged into the User Interface.
Tenant#1 allows access to the Campaign and Series pages.
Tenant#2 allows access to the Admin pages.
Press the Continue button to open the QALIPSIS dashboard.

chapter.title: Navigating the QALIPSIS Interface
chapter.simpara: When you are logged into QALIPSIS, a left sidebar is displayed and remains displayed throughout your session. The menu near the top of the sidebar depends on the tenant that you are using.

listitem.simpara: Click on your name near the bottom of the sidebar to open the Tenant Modal window.
Select an availablet tenant to use for the current session.
With Tenant #1 selected, the Campaign and Series screens are accessible by clicking on the appropriate link near the top of the sidebar.
With Tenant #2 selected, the Admin area of the interface is accessible (if you have Admin permissions).

section.title: View/Update Your profile
section.simpara: From the Tenant #1 sidebar:

listitem.simpara: Click on your name at the bottom of the left sidebar to open the Tenant Modal window.
Click on the settings icon.
Click on My profile to open the My Profile page.
Make changes to your profile and click on Save changes.

section.title: Change your password
section.simpara: From the Tenant #1 sidebar:

listitem.simpara: Click on your name at the bottom of the left sidebar to open the Tenant Modal window.
Click on the settings icon.
Click on My profile to open the My Profile page.
Click on Change password.
Enter your new password in the New Password and Comfirm Password fields.
Click om Save changes. to save your new password.
click on your name at the bottom of the left sidebar to open the Tenant Modal window.
Click on the settings icon.
Click the Logout button. You will be logged out and returned to the Welcome screen.

section.title: Access Campaign pages
section.simpara: The Campaign screen is accessible from the Tenant #2 left sidebar. The Campaign screen allows the user to view current campaign data, update a campaign, and create a new campaign. Refer to and for more details.

section.title: Access Series pages
section.simpara: The Series screen is accessible from the Tenant #2 left sidebar. The Series' screen allows the user view current series information, update or duplicate a series, and create a new series.
Refer to , , and for more details.

section.title: Access Admin Functions
section.simpara: From the Tenant2 sidebar (if you have Admin permissions): . Click on the Admin button to display the Users screen. . Click on a user’s name to open the Update User window.
From the Update User window, user information can be updated and modified and the Roles (permissions) set.
More options will be available from the Admin area of the GUI in the near future.

chapter.title: Creating and updating a series
chapter.simpara: You can create a series if your profile has write permissions. You can update a series if you are the owner and/or have write permissions.

listitem.simpara: Login as Tenant #1.
Click on Series in the left sidebar menu.
The Series page opens and displays a table listing currently defined series by name, Type…
Click on the ‘+New series’ button near the top right corner of the page.
The New series modal window opens.
Enter the required data into the appropriate fields.
Click on the Create button.
The following text is displayed in the modal window: '[Series Name] has just been created!'.
Close the New series modal window to return to the Series page.
The newly created series is displayed in the table.

chapter.simpara: You can update a series if you are the owner and/or have write permissions.

listitem.simpara: Login as Tenant #1.
Click on Series in the left sidebar menu.
The Series page opens and displays a table listing currently defined series by name, Type…
Click on athe name of the series you want to update.
The New series modal window opens.
Enter the required data into the appropriate fields.
Click on the Create button.
The following text is displayed in the modal window: '[Series Name] has just been created!'.
Close the New series modal window to return to the Series page.
The newly created series is displayed in the table.
Click on the +Campaign button near the top right of the Campaign page.
A page will open displaying the campaign name, a settings icon, a search field, and a table listing scenarios that are available to run in the campaign.
Optionally, rename the campaign by hovering over the New Campaign title and selecting the pen icon to edit the name.
Select the checkbox to the left of each scenario that you want to include in your campaign.
Note that each scenarios listed has a default configuration.
If you want to view and/or update the scenario to better suit your test, click on the Custom Settings buttom to the right of the scenario.
‘Changes are saved for the current campaign and doesn’t affect the default Series settings'
Optionally, configure the canpaign with custom run-time values.
Click on the arrow next to the ‘Run immediately’ button at the bottom of the page.
The custom running modal window opens.
Select the date you want to start the campaign using the datepicker.
The date format is [Month dd, yyyy]
Select the time zone for the campaign start time from the ‘Time zone’ dropdown.
The time zone format is [(UTC+hh:mm) Сontinent/ City].
If you want the campaign to run more than once, select a frequecy from the [Frequency] drop down.
The frequecy options are Daily, Weekly, and Monthly.
Daily runs the campaign at the previoulsy selected time each day.
Weekly runs the campaign on the selected day(s) every week at the previoulsy selected time of day.
Monthly runs the campaign each selected day(s) of every month at the previously selected time.
Login to QALIPSIS as Tenant #1.
Click on Campaign in the left sidebar.
A table listing all existing and planned campaigns is displayed.
Click on any column title to sort the table by ascending/descending order.
Search for a specific campaign by clicking on the magnifying glass icon near the top right of the screen and entering the name (or partial name) of the campaign.
Click on the name of a campaign to display the Campaign details page.
A report box is displayed at the top of the screen.
The top line in the report box displays information for the whole campaign; the following lines display information for each scenario within the campaign.
Click the View more tooltip on the right side of the report box to expand the report box to display all scenarios in the campaign.
Click the More details tooltip to display the info for the total campaign in a dropdown box.
Click on the Campiagn Summary dropdown filter to select the scenario information to display in the report box.
Below the report box, a row of filter cards is displayed with the individual events and meters that are logged for the campaign.
Refer to Step Specifications for guidance creating filtering steps.
Each card includes the Name, Type, and display Color.

Below the row of filter cards, a chart is displayed with the x-axis displaying time and the y-axis displaying minion count. Select one or more series cards to display the generated data for that series.

entry.simpara: A step within a scenario that perform cross-checks of data and durations from different sources.
A collection of values, as opposed to a single value.
A single execution of one or a collection of scenarios, starting at a set time and running for a set period.
A set of pieces/nodes of the software that are executed in different locations or on different machines.

A technique in which an object receives other objects that it depends on, called dependencies. A dependency injection involves four objects each having a different role: * The service object that is injected into a client. * The client object that receives the injected service. * The interface object that defines how the client uses the service. * The injector object that constructs the services and injects them into the client.
A subpart of a scenario made up of a single continuous sequence of steps, to be executed in the same location by the same minions.

listitem.simpara: Root DAG: A DAG starting immediately under a scenario
Frontend DAG: A DAG whose followers execute on a different factory.
Backend DAG: A DAG that continues the execution workflow of a DAG hosted in a different factory.
DAG Underload: A DAG that is executed by the number of minions set on the scenario.
Singleton DAG: A DAG that is executed only once and supports infrastructure or optimization operations.

entry.simpara: A single instruction published by a head to trigger an action in a factory.
A time-stamped snapshot within a step. There are several types of events that can be monitored, including the time to respond, the status of the response, the size of the payload to send, etc.
The factory reply to a directive; the feedback provides status of the directive’s progression and/or the result of the expected action.
The output of the previous step that is used in the current step’s execution.
A branch of a scenario that receives load from all of the minions.

listitem.simpara: A meter is a value aggregating step of the performance over a short period of time across all the minions.
A step meter is a unique value representing the performance of a unique execution of a step by a minion (e.g., time to response or volume of data saved, etc.)

entry.simpara: A virtual actor simulating a website user, an IoT device, or another system that interacts with the test system to inject load and verify performance.
A set of records generated by the execution of a campaign that permit the analysis of the performance of the target system and the generation of dashboards.
A software entity of a QALIPSIS cluster. Nodes communicate with each other to exchange data.

listitem.simpara: Head: Node of a QALIPSIS cluster that coordinates and maintains the state of the cluster, orchestrating the factories and the campaigns.
Factory: Node of a QALIPSIS cluster that executes the scenario to generate load to a target system and verify its behavior.

entry.simpara: A command that allows you create or configure a scenario or a step.
Any data that a step generates, which may be used to verify behavior or generate further load.
A technical extension designed to extend the core capabilities of QALIPSIS.
An operator that defines the pace that the minions are injected into the system. QALIPSIS out of the box ramping operators include:

listitem.simpara: Timeframe
More
Faster
Regular
Step report: A consolidated view of the executions of a step.
Campaign report: A consolidated view of the step reports.

entry.simpara: The record returned by a step when executed by a minion. The result contains values (e.g., records fetched from a database or a response to a request) and the step meters of the execution. The result is dispatched as output of the step context to the followers.

A component in charge of triggering and orchestrating the flow of data from one step to the next. The runner is responsible for ensuring that output data from each step is provided as input to the next step as appropriate. Each runner task is assigned to the minion that simulates the user or device executing it.
Scenario parameters that are set at run-time or execution of the scenario.
Sequence of actions to be executed by minions or to poll data from a remote system.
The Key/Value pair attached to a step to locate its execution in a factory owning the same pair.
The configuration of a step or a scenario that describes the actions to perform and the sequence in which they are to be performed.

The central atomic components within each scenario. Steps are the challenges minions must complete in order to continue in the scenario. Each step has purpose. There are several step types within QALIPSIS:

listitem.simpara: Error processing steps that contains information for handling a failed assertion or execution.

Proxy steps that act as a middle man to forward the step execution to a follower, using convenient strategies. Operator steps that act on its data input or output before releasing it to the next step,
Assertion steps that validate incoming or outgoing data.

entry.simpara: A single execution of a single step by a single minion. minion * Execution: A set of fields and values representing the single execution of a single minion on a single step.

listitem.simpara: Exhausted: After an execution failure, a step context is marked as exhausted, and this state is propagated to its descendants.
Tail: A step context that represents the last one in the execution workflow of a single minion.

entry.simpara: An IT system that QALIPSIS injects load into in order to verify its ability to scale and support a high volume of users or data.
